elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate Gibbs measures for diffusive particles interacting through a two-body mean field energy.
elements.sent-2411-002: By uncovering a gradient structure for the conditional law, we derive sharp bounds on the size of chaos, providing a quantitative characterization of particle independence.
elements.sent-2411-003: To handle unbounded interaction forces, we study the concentration of measure phenomenon for Gibbs measures via a defective Talagrand inequality, which may hold independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our approach provides a unified framework for both the flat semi-convex and displacement convex cases.
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, we establish sharp chaos bounds for the quartic Curie--Weiss model in the sub-critical regime, demonstrating the generality of this method.
elements.sent-2411-006: Let $d$ be an integer $\geqslant 1$ and let $\X$ be the $d$-dimensional torus $\T^d$ or the Euclidean space $\R^d$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Let $V \colon \X \to \R$ and $W \colon \X \times \X \to \R$ be $\mathcal C^1$ and suppose that $W$ is symmetric.
elements.sent-2411-008: We call $V$, $W$ the confinement and interaction potentials respectively.
elements.sent-2411-009: The central object of this paper is the $N$-particle Gibbs measure $m^N_*$, defined by \begin{equation}m^N_*(\dd x^1\cdots\dd x^N) = \frac{\exp \bigl( - \sum_{i \in [N]} V(x^i) - \frac 1{2N} \sum_{i,j \in [N]} W(x^i,x^j) \bigr) \dd x^1\cdots\dd x^N} {\int_{\X^N}\exp \bigl( - \sum_{i \in [N]} V(y^i) - \frac 1{2N} \sum_{i,j \in [N]} W(y^i,y^j) \bigr) \dd y^1\cdots\dd y^N},\end{equation} where the $1/N$ factor represents the mean field scaling for interactions between the $N$ particles.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}m^N_*(\dd x^1\cdots\dd x^N) = \frac{\exp \bigl( - \sum_{i \in [N]} V(x^i) - \frac 1{2N} \sum_{i,j \in [N]} W(x^i,x^j) \bigr) \dd x^1\cdots\dd x^N} {\int_{\X^N}\exp \bigl( - \sum_{i \in [N]} V(y^i) - \frac 1{2N} \sum_{i,j \in [N]} W(y^i,y^j) \bigr) \dd y^1\cdots\dd y^N},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-010: In the case $\X = \R^d$, we assume in addition that the term in the exponent is upper bounded and that the measure $m^N_*$ is well defined for $N \geqslant 1$.
elements.sent-2411-011: We also assume throughout the paper that there exists a unique mean field Gibbs measure $m_* \in \mathcal P(\X)$ solving the fixed point equation \begin{equation}\label{eq:m*-fixed-point} m_* = \Pi [m_*],\end{equation} where the mapping $\Pi$ between probability measures is defined by \begin{equation}\label{eq:def-Pi} \Pi[m](\dd x) = \frac{\exp\bigl( -V(x) - \langle W(x,\cdot), m\rangle\bigr)\dd x} {\int_{\X} \exp\bigl( -V(y) - \langle W(y,\cdot), m\rangle \dd y}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:def-Pi} \Pi[m](\dd x) = \frac{\exp\bigl( -V(x) - \langle W(x,\cdot), m\rangle\bigr)\dd x} {\int_{\X} \exp\bigl( -V(y) - \langle W(y,\cdot), m\rangle \dd y}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-012: Here $\langle \cdot, \cdot\rangle$ denotes the integral of a function with respect to a measure.
elements.sent-2411-013: For $\X = \R^d$, we suppose that the measure $\Pi[m]$ is well defined for all $m \in \mathcal P_2(\R^d)$ and $m_* \in \mathcal P_2(\R^d)$.
elements.sent-2411-014: Through formal computations, it can be verified that $m^N_*$ is the invariant measure of the $N$-particle interacting diffusion process \begin{equation}\dd X^i_t = - \nabla V(X^i_t) \dd t - \frac 1N \sum_{j \in [N]} \nabla_1 W(X^i_t, X^j_t) \dd t + \sqrt 2 \dd B^i_t,\qquad i \in [N].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\dd X^i_t = - \nabla V(X^i_t) \dd t - \frac 1N \sum_{j \in [N]} \nabla_1 W(X^i_t, X^j_t) \dd t + \sqrt 2 \dd B^i_t,\qquad i \in [N].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-015: The mean field Gibbs measure $m_*$ can also be shown to be invariant under the non-linear diffusion process \begin{equation}\label{eq:mfl} \dd X_t = - \nabla V(X_t) \dd t - \langle \nabla_1 W(X_t, \cdot), m_t\rangle \dd t + \sqrt 2 \dd B_t, \qquad m_t = \Law(X_t).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq:mfl} \dd X_t = - \nabla V(X_t) \dd t - \langle \nabla_1 W(X_t, \cdot), m_t\rangle \dd t + \sqrt 2 \dd B_t, \qquad m_t = \Law(X_t).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-016: In these equations, $B^i_t$ and $B_t$ are independent $d$-dimensional Brownian motions.

elements.sent-2411-018: The main purpose of this paper is to investigate the quantitative chaotic behavior of the sequence $m^N_*$ towards $m_*$.
elements.sent-2411-019: To establish our framework, let $k \geqslant 1$ and for all $N \geqslant k$, we define the marginal distributions \begin{equation}m^{N,k}_*(\cdot) = \int_{\X^{N-k}} m^N_*(\cdot,\dd x^{k+1}\cdots\dd x^N).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-020: We say the sequence $(m^N_*)_{N \geqslant 1}$ is $m_*$-chaotic if for all $k \geqslant 1$, the weak convergence \begin{equation}m^{N,k}_* \rightharpoonup m_*^{\otimes k}\end{equation} holds when $N \to \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-021: The classical notion of chaos, introduced by Kac \cite{KacFoundations , is fundamental in the study of large particle systems.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Demonstrating its propagation over time is a key objective of kinetic theory, as it provides a physical basis for partial differential equations describing the system's evolution.
elements.sent-2411-023: Notable contributions to qualitative chaos include Sznitman's martingale approach \cite{SznitmanBoltzmannHomogenes to the Boltzmann--Kac particle system and the analysis by Fournier, Hauray and Mischler \cite{FHMVortex on the 2D viscous vortex model.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: See \cite[Section~4.2]{ChaintronDiezPOC1 for a detailed review of these results.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our aim is to go beyond this qualitative convergence and instead quantify the disparity between the two probability measures $m^{N,k _*$ and $m_*^{\otimes k}$ for arbitrary $k$ and $N$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: This quantitative problem is referred to as the size of chaos in the recent literatures \cite{PPSChaos,DuerinckxChaos .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Following the approach of Lacker \cite{LackerQuantitative , we consider the local relative entropy: \begin{equation}H(m^{N,k}_* | m_*^{\otimes k}) \coloneqq \int_{\X^k} \log \frac{m^{N,k}_*(\x^{[k]})}{m_*^{\otimes k}(\x^{[k]})} m^{N,k}_* (\dd\x^{[k]}),\end{equation} where the measures are identified with their densities and $\x^{[k]}$ denotes the $k$-tuple $(x^1,\ldots,x^k)$.}
elements.sent-2411-028: A key advantage of relative entropy is its linear scaling with the number of particles.
elements.sent-2411-029: More specifically, it satisfies the subaddivity property: \begin{equation}H(m^{N,k}_* | m_*^{\otimes k}) \leqslant \frac{1}{\lfloor N/k\rfloor} H(m^{N}_* | m_*^{\otimes N}),\end{equation} $\lfloor\cdot\rfloor$ denoting the integer part.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}H(m^{N,k}_* | m_*^{\otimes k}) \leqslant \frac{1}{\lfloor N/k\rfloor} H(m^{N}_* | m_*^{\otimes N}),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-030: In scenarios involving weak interaction potential $W$, the global entropy typically exhibits the asympototic behavior \begin{equation}H(m^{N}_* | m_*^{\otimes N}) = O(1)\end{equation} when $N\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Consequently, by subadditivity, \begin{equation}H(m^{N,k}_* | m_*^{\otimes k}) = O\biggl( \frac kN \biggr).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: This result not only provides a quantitative bound on the size of chaos but also establishes $m_*$-chaoticity of the sequence $m^N_*$, as $k/N \to 0$ when $N \to \infty$ for fixed $k$.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, this global-to-local strategy breaks down for stronger metrics such as $L^2$ or $L^p$ norms, which do not exhibit the linear scaling property.
elements.sent-2411-034: In those cases, a purely local approach is necessary to establish chaoticity, as exemplified in the recent works of Bresch--Jabin--Soler \cite{BJSNewApproach and Hess-Childs--Rowan \cite{HCRHigher .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: While the global-local transition in the entropy method offers conceptual and technical convenience, the subadditivity fails to yield optimal bounds for the size of chaos, even in elementary Gaussian scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-036: Lacker has demonstrated the limitation both for Gibbs measures \cite[Example~2.3]{LackerQuantitative and for stochastic processes \cite[Section~3]{LackerHier .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Specifically, the sharp chaos bound for Gaussian variables is instead the following: \begin{equation}H(m^{N,k}_* | m_*^{\otimes k}) = O\biggl( \frac {k^2}{N^2} \biggr),\end{equation} indicating that the passage to local entropy incurs a loss of exponents when $k/N = o(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-038: In \cite{LackerQuantitative,LackerHier , Lacker develops techniques based on the BBGKY hierarchy and log-Sobolev inequalities to establish this sharp chaos bound for a wide class of regular potentials $V$ and $W$, demonstrating the genericity of the $O(k^2\!/N^2)$ bound.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Several subsequent works have extended these results in the dynamical setting.
elements.sent-2411-040: First, Lacker and Le Flem \cite{LLFSharp show that, under a weak and regular interaction potential, the size of chaos remains uniformly bounded in time.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Han \cite{HanEnt extends these results to divergence-free and $L^p$ drift kernels for $p > d$, albeit with strong restrictions on the initial data.}
elements.sent-2411-042: One of the authors \cite{slpoc removes these restrictions, proving sharp propagation of chaos for the broader $W^{-1,\infty}$ class in high viscosity and achieving time-uniform estimates in the divergence-free case.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Hess-Childs and Rowan \cite{HCRHigher , focusing on bounded drift kernels, construct an impressive power series expansion for the $N$-particle evolution in terms of $1/N$, showing that the size of the $n$-th perturbative order is $O(k^{2n}\!/N^{2n )$.}}

elements.sent-2411-049: This approach constructs a hierarchy of marginal Fisher informations: \begin{equation}I(m^{N,k}_*|m_*^{\otimes k}) = \int_{\X^k} \biggl| \nabla \log \frac{m^{N,k}_*(\x^{[k]})} {m_*^{\otimes k}(\x^{[k]})} \biggr|^2 m^{N,k}_*(\dd\x^{[k]}), \qquad k \leqslant N,\end{equation} where controlling growth across levels requires weak interactions, specifically the smallness of $W$.
elements.sent-2411-050: To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel methodology based on a hierarchy of conditional entropies, which we outline below.
elements.sent-2411-051: Our approach centers on analyzing the conditional measure \begin{equation}m^{N,k|[k-1]}_{*,\x^{[k-1]}} \coloneqq \frac{m^{N,k+1}_*(\x^{[k+1]})}{m_*^{N,k}(\x^{[k]})},\end{equation} which represents the law of the $k$-th particle conditioned on the configurations of the first $k-1$ particles.
elements.sent-2411-052: The average conditional entropy of this measure can be expressed using the chain rule as: \begin{equation}\int_{\X^{k-1}} H(m^{N,k|[k-1]}_{*,\x^{[k-1]}}|m_*) \, m^{N,k-1}_*(\dd\x^{[k-1]}) = H(m^{N,k}_*|m_*^{\otimes k}) - H(m^{N,k-1}_*|m_*^{\otimes (k-1)}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-053: Thus we consider a higher-order hierarchy than that used in Lacker's approach.
elements.sent-2411-054: At equilibrium, this conditional law is characterized by two forces: the interaction among the first $k$ particles, encoded by the empirical measure \begin{equation}\mu_{\x^{[k]}} \coloneqq \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i \in [k]} \delta_{x^i},\end{equation} and the interaction with the remaining $N-k$ particles, encoded in the $(k+1)$-th-level conditional measure \begin{equation}m^{N,k+1|[k]}_{*,\x^{[k]}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-055: We hypothesize that these two conditional measures of neighboring levels are closely aligned.
elements.sent-2411-056: A direct calculation confirms that their average relative entropy corresponds to the second-order entropy difference: \begin{equation}\int_{\X^k} H(m^{N,k+1|[k]}_{*,\x^{[k]}} | m^{N,k|[k-1]}_{*,\x^{[k-1]}}) \, m^{N,k}_*(\dd\x^{[k]}) = H(m^{N,k+1}_*|m_*^{\otimes (k+1)}) - 2H(m^{N,k}_*|m_*^{\otimes k}) + H(m^{N,k-1}_*|m_*^{\otimes (k-1)}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-057: This higher-order error term allows for a more manageable hierarchical structure, whose qualitative behavior is unaffected by the magnitude of $W$.
elements.sent-2411-058: Furthermore, the comparison of these conditional laws introduces the non-linear Fisher information \begin{equation}I(m^{N,k|[k-1]}_{*,\x^{[k-1]}} | \Pi[m^{N,k|[k-1]}_{*,\x^{[k-1]}}])\end{equation} as a dissipation term, which is directly connected to the free energy landscape of the non-linear diffusion process \eqref{eq:mfl .}
elements.sent-2411-059: This observation enables us to establish sharp chaos bounds in various scenarios where the dissipation structure of the mean field gradient dynamics is well understood.
